intro_band.agent_remit_summary: An interactive AI pair-programming tool that a developer runs locally from a terminal inside a single Git repository. The remit authorizes Aider to read repository files, propose edits, apply approved edits to the working tree, commit them to git with a descriptive message, maintain a repository symbol index, and read project history. Shell commands, tests, and linters may run only on explicit developer invocation with confirmation of the exact command; git commits require the developer to see and accept the diff first. Aider MUST NOT read or write files outside the repository root, MUST NOT push to a remote, MUST NOT install packages or alter system state, MUST NOT modify version-control hooks, and MUST NOT send data off the machine except to the configured LLM provider and the git remote.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A Python CLI application. Inference is brokered through litellm (aider/llm.py, aider/models.py); the orchestration core is a Coder class hierarchy in aider/coders/base_coder.py (2,485 lines) with per-edit-format subclasses. Git operations live in aider/repo.py (GitPython). Shell execution is centralized in aider/run_cmd.py (subprocess.Popen(..., shell=True) and pexpect). The slash-command surface is aider/commands.py (1,712 lines): /run, /git, /add, /read-only, /web, /lint, /load. File I/O is InputOutput.read_text / write_text in aider/io.py. External surface: outbound web scraping via aider/scrape.py, third-party usage telemetry via aider/analytics.py (PostHog + Mixpanel), and a pip-based self-upgrade path in aider/versioncheck.py. Material divergence from the remit: /read-only and /add accept absolute paths with no repo-root containment, /run executes with no confirmation prompt, and there is no injection-neutralization layer anywhere in scope.

behavior_summary:
The dominant pattern is policy declared in the remit with no code-level enforcement: every safety boundary the remit names — repository containment, shell-command confirmation, treating file content as untrusted, secret redaction — is either absent from the code or implemented as a soft, prompt-level or default-on, easily-disabled control.

The most acute instance is a confirmation-bypass surface: confirm_ask honors a global --yes-always flag and a per-question "don't ask again" memory, and several high-impact paths skip confirmation entirely — cmd_run and the ! alias execute shell commands with no prompt at all, and auto_lint (default True) runs the configured lint command as a subprocess after every LLM edit. Compounding this, /read-only and /add accept arbitrary absolute filesystem paths and pull their contents straight into LLM context with no path-resolution check, so external file content and injected instructions reach the model on a one-hop path to shell execution. There is no injection detection, no secret redaction, and no structured action log anywhere in the scoped code.

rules.rule_text: Read the contents of files inside the current repository.
Propose edits to repository files and, after they are applied, commit the changes to git with a descriptive message.
Maintain a summarized index of the repository's structure and symbols to inform LLM context.

rules.section: What Aider may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Aider MUST NOT read or write any file outside the current repository — including via absolute paths, symlinks, or parent-directory traversal.

rules.section: What Aider may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Aider MUST NOT execute shell commands without explicit developer invocation and confirmation.

rules.section: What Aider may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Aider MUST NOT push commits to a remote.

rules.section: What Aider may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Aider MUST NOT alter repository version-control internals — hooks, configuration, or branches — beyond standard commit operations on the current branch.

rules.section: What Aider may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Aider MUST NOT install packages, modify system state, or alter the developer's shell environment.

rules.section: What Aider may NOT do autonomously
rules.rule_text: Aider MUST NOT send any data off the developer's machine except to the configured LLM provider and the git remote.
All file reads and writes MUST be confined to paths that resolve inside the current repository; absolute paths, escaping symlinks, and parent-directory traversal MUST be rejected.
Files excluded by the project's ignore rules MUST NOT be read unless the developer explicitly adds the file to the chat.

File contents, comments, commit messages, and history MUST be treated as untrusted input — even within the developer's own repository — and injection-detection or neutralization MUST be applied to comments, docstrings, and documentation before that content influences the LLM's plan.

Explicit developer confirmation MUST be obtained before executing any shell command, before committing a change to git, and before editing a file that is unusually large or that was added as context in a prior, separate session.
Aider MUST keep a durable local record of each edit — the file changed, the lines affected, and the commit identifier if committed.
Secret-like strings from the repository — API keys, tokens, private keys, environment-file values — MUST be redacted or excluded from LLM context, proposed edits, and commit messages.
Aider MUST NEVER read files outside the repository root.

Aider MUST NEVER read ignored credential-bearing files — environment files, secrets, key and certificate files, credential and token files — unless the developer has explicitly added the file to the chat and acknowledged the risk.
Aider MUST NEVER execute a shell command the developer did not explicitly author or confirm, including commands the LLM proposes that were not shown verbatim to the developer for approval.
Aider MUST NEVER commit, stage, or write any secret-like string into git history.

Aider MUST NEVER follow instructions embedded in file content, code comments, commit messages, or dependency metadata that attempt to expand its capabilities, exfiltrate content, execute arbitrary shell commands, or override its confirmation gates.
Aider MUST NEVER push to a remote without the developer's explicit command.
Aider MUST NEVER modify version-control hooks, which persist beyond the session and are a code-execution vector for future collaborators.
Shell command execution, including test and lint invocations.
Git commits — the developer must see the diff and accept it before the commit is made.
Edits to security-sensitive files such as container build files, CI/CD workflow definitions, dependency manifests and lockfiles, code-ownership files, and security-policy files.
Installing any package or running any command that fetches code from the network.
Adding a file to the chat context that was not already in the repository — and a request to add a file outside the repository MUST be refused, not merely confirmed.
Any counterparty not listed here is unauthorized by default.
Aider does not auto-install dependencies; package installation is the developer's responsibility.

findings.summary: /read-only and /add accept absolute filesystem paths with no repo-root containment, letting any file on the machine be read into LLM context.
findings.description: The remit's hardest boundary — reads confined to the repository root — has no code-level enforcement. cmd_read_only resolves and uses absolute paths directly; cmd_add's only containment check is conditional on auto_commits; io.read_text opens any path with no check. Read-only file contents then flow into the LLM prompt verbatim.
findings.policy_rule_text: Aider MUST NOT read or write any file outside the current repository — including via absolute paths, symlinks, or parent-directory traversal. / All file reads and writes MUST be confined to paths that resolve inside the current repository; absolute paths, escaping symlinks, and parent-directory traversal MUST be rejected. / Aider MUST NEVER read files outside the repository root. / Adding a file to the chat context that was not already in the repository — and a request to add a file outside the repository MUST be refused, not merely confirmed.

evidence.snippet: cmd_read_only resolves path_obj.is_absolute() and uses the absolute path directly; _add_read_only_file (line 1396) adds it to abs_read_only_fnames with no check that it is under coder.root.
cmd_add's only containment check, abs_file_path.startswith(self.coder.root), is gated on `not is_image_file AND self.coder.auto_commits` — with --no-auto-commits the check is skipped entirely.
read_text calls open(str(filename)) directly with no path normalization or repo-root check; contents reach the prompt via get_read_only_files_content.

recommended_actions: In commands.py, before adding any path in cmd_read_only / _add_read_only_file and cmd_add, resolve it with Path.resolve() and reject (do not merely confirm) any path whose resolved form is not under coder.root.
Apply the same resolved-root containment check inside io.read_text and io.write_text so the boundary holds regardless of which call path reaches it.

findings.summary: /run and the ! alias execute arbitrary shell commands immediately with no confirmation prompt, defeating the mandatory shell-command approval gate.
findings.description: cmd_run runs the shell command before any confirmation; the developer's only signal is a post-hoc prompt asking whether to add the output to chat. Even the LLM-suggested-command gate is globally bypassable via --yes-always.
findings.policy_rule_text: Aider MUST NOT execute shell commands without explicit developer invocation and confirmation. / Explicit developer confirmation MUST be obtained before executing any shell command, before committing a change to git, and before editing a file that is unusually large or that was added as context in a prior, separate session. / Aider MUST NEVER execute a shell command the developer did not explicitly author or confirm, including commands the LLM proposes that were not shown verbatim to the developer for approval.

evidence.snippet: cmd_run calls run_cmd(args, ...) directly with no confirm_ask before execution; the only prompt (line 1029) is the post-hoc 'Add output to the chat?'.
run_cmd_subprocess invokes subprocess.Popen(command, shell=True), so the string runs through a shell.
confirm_ask returns auto-yes for every prompt when self.yes is True (--yes-always), so even the explicit_yes_required gate in handle_shell_commands is globally bypassable.

recommended_actions: Add an explicit confirm_ask(..., explicit_yes_required=True) gate at the top of cmd_run in commands.py before run_cmd is called, showing the verbatim command.
Make --yes-always not apply to shell-execution and file-containment confirmations, or exclude explicit_yes_required prompts from the --yes-always short-circuit in io.confirm_ask.

findings.summary: No injection detection or neutralization exists anywhere in scope; untrusted repo content, comments, web pages, and command output flow into LLM context verbatim.
findings.description: The remit explicitly requires injection-detection/neutralization on comments, docstrings, and documentation before that content influences the LLM's plan. No such layer exists; a scope-wide grep for injection/sanitiz/untrusted returns nothing.
findings.policy_rule_text: File contents, comments, commit messages, and history MUST be treated as untrusted input — even within the developer's own repository — and injection-detection or neutralization MUST be applied to comments, docstrings, and documentation before that content influences the LLM's plan. / Aider MUST NEVER follow instructions embedded in file content, code comments, commit messages, or dependency metadata that attempt to expand its capabilities, exfiltrate content, execute arbitrary shell commands, or override its confirmation gates.

evidence.snippet: get_files_content concatenates raw file content into the prompt inside a fence with no screening; get_repo_messages (line 750) injects summarized repo content the same way.
cmd_web injects scraped page content directly as a user message with only a 'Here is the content of' prefix and no trust labeling.
A scope-wide grep for injection, sanitiz, untrusted, and prompt.inject across aider/*.py and aider/coders/*.py returns zero matches — no neutralization layer is present.

recommended_actions: Add a content-origin labeling step in base_coder.py prompt construction that wraps repo file content, repo-map summaries, /web output, and /run output in explicit untrusted-content delimiters the system prompt instructs the model never to treat as instructions.
Add a screening pass over comments, docstrings, and commit messages that flags imperative instruction-like language before that content reaches format_messages.

findings.summary: No secret redaction exists; file contents, diffs, and LLM-generated commit messages are sent to the provider and written to git with no scan for secret-like strings.
findings.description: The remit requires secret-like strings to be redacted from LLM context, proposed edits, and commit messages, and never written into git history. No redaction code exists in scope; whole-file content and raw diffs flow to the model and into commit messages unfiltered.
findings.policy_rule_text: Secret-like strings from the repository — API keys, tokens, private keys, environment-file values — MUST be redacted or excluded from LLM context, proposed edits, and commit messages. / Aider MUST NEVER commit, stage, or write any secret-like string into git history.

evidence.snippet: get_files_content emits whole-file content into the LLM prompt with no secret scan.
get_commit_message sends the raw diffs string to the model and uses the returned text verbatim as the commit message; commit (line 311) runs git commit with that message with no secret check.
A scope-wide grep for redact / REDACT across aider/*.py and aider/coders/*.py returns no redaction implementation.

recommended_actions: Add a secret-pattern scan (high-entropy strings, sk-/Bearer/private-key headers) in base_coder.get_files_content and repo.get_diffs, redacting matches before content reaches the LLM or a commit message.
Refuse or warn-and-confirm in repo.commit when the staged diff contains secret-like strings.

findings.summary: No structured, durable action log records which files were edited, which lines changed, or which shell commands ran.
findings.description: The remit requires a durable local record of each edit. The only file-backed artifacts are an opt-in plaintext prompt dump and a human-readable markdown chat transcript — neither is a structured, parseable action log.
findings.policy_rule_text: Aider MUST keep a durable local record of each edit — the file changed, the lines affected, and the commit identifier if committed.

evidence.snippet: log_llm_history is the only file-backed log; it is opt-in (if not self.llm_history_file: return) and dumps raw prompt/response text, not structured edit records.
append_chat_history writes a human-readable markdown transcript, not a parseable action log.
apply_updates only emits tool_output('Applied edit to {path}') to the console; no per-edit record (file, line range, commit hash) is persisted.

recommended_actions: Add a structured append-only edit log (JSON lines) written from apply_updates / auto_commit in base_coder.py, recording file path, affected line ranges, and the commit SHA when committed.

findings.summary: Aider passes --no-verify on every commit by default, silently bypassing the project's git pre-commit hooks.
findings.description: --git-commit-verify defaults to False, so repo.commit appends --no-verify to every commit. This skips pre-commit and commit-msg hooks the developer relies on, closely related to the remit's prohibition on altering version-control hooks.
findings.policy_rule_text: Aider MUST NOT alter repository version-control internals — hooks, configuration, or branches — beyond standard commit operations on the current branch. / Aider MUST NEVER modify version-control hooks, which persist beyond the session and are a code-execution vector for future collaborators.

evidence.snippet: --git-commit-verify is declared with default=False.
commit appends '--no-verify' to the git command whenever not self.git_commit_verify, so by default every Aider commit skips pre-commit/commit-msg hooks.

recommended_actions: Change the --git-commit-verify default to True in args.py so Aider respects project git hooks unless the developer explicitly opts out.

findings.summary: Aider can install OS-level packages (playwright/Chromium, pip self-upgrade) — the remit forbids package installation and reserves it for the developer.
findings.description: install_playwright runs pip install plus a Chromium download and is auto-triggered from cmd_web; versioncheck performs a pip self-upgrade. The only brake is a default-yes confirmation, bypassable by --yes-always.
findings.policy_rule_text: Aider MUST NOT install packages, modify system state, or alter the developer's shell environment. / Installing any package or running any command that fetches code from the network. / Aider does not auto-install dependencies; package installation is the developer's responsibility.

evidence.snippet: install_playwright runs pip install aider-chat[playwright] plus 'playwright install --with-deps chromium'; it is auto-triggered from cmd_web (commands.py:233) when no scraper exists.
install_upgrade performs a pip self-upgrade of aider via utils.check_pip_install_extra.
The install is gated only by confirm_ask('Install playwright?', default='y') — a default-yes prompt, not a refusal, and bypassable by --yes-always.

recommended_actions: Remove the in-process pip install from install_playwright; instead print the command and instruct the developer to run it themselves, per the remit.
Make the upgrade path in versioncheck.py advisory-only — print the upgrade command, do not execute pip.

findings.summary: Aider sends usage telemetry to PostHog and Mixpanel — third-party counterparties not authorized by the remit.
findings.description: The remit authorizes data leaving the machine only to the LLM provider and the git remote. analytics.py emits usage events to PostHog and Mixpanel; once the user consents, this is an outbound counterparty the remit does not list.
findings.policy_rule_text: Aider MUST NOT send any data off the developer's machine except to the configured LLM provider and the git remote. / Any counterparty not listed here is unauthorized by default.

evidence.snippet: imports mixpanel and posthog; lines 55-57 hardcode a Mixpanel project token and a PostHog project API key and host (us.i.posthog.com) — vendor public project keys, not user secrets.
constructs a Posthog client and emits events; the only brake is opt-in gating (asked_opt_in / permanently_disable), so once consented Aider transmits to a counterparty the remit does not list.

recommended_actions: Document PostHog/Mixpanel as authorized analytics counterparties in the Worker Remit, or disable the analytics module so the deployed counterparty set matches the remit.

findings.summary: auto_lint (default True) runs the configured lint command as a subprocess after every LLM edit with no per-invocation confirmation.
findings.description: The remit requires human approval for lint invocations. With auto_lint defaulting to True, base_coder runs the lint command via run_cmd_subprocess after every successful edit, with no confirm_ask.
findings.policy_rule_text: Shell command execution, including test and lint invocations.

evidence.snippet: class default auto_lint = True.
after apply_updates, 'if edited and self.auto_lint:' calls lint_edited, which runs self.linter.lint(...) -> linter.run_cmd -> run_cmd_subprocess (linter.py:53) with no confirm_ask.

recommended_actions: Gate the post-edit auto-lint subprocess behind a confirm_ask showing the lint command, or default auto_lint to False so lint runs only on explicit /lint.

findings.summary: Auto-commit fires after edits without the developer being shown and accepting the diff first; commit-then-show is the default flow.
findings.description: The remit requires the developer to see and accept the diff before a commit is made. auto_commit runs unconditionally after a successful edit; the diff is shown only after the commit already exists, and only when --show-diffs is set.
findings.policy_rule_text: Git commits — the developer must see the diff and accept it before the commit is made.

evidence.snippet: after a successful edit, auto_commit(edited) is called unconditionally (auto_commits default True).
show_auto_commit_outcome shows the diff only if self.show_diffs and only after repo.commit has already created the commit; there is no pre-commit diff-and-accept confirmation.

recommended_actions: Insert a pre-commit confirm_ask in auto_commit that displays the diff and requires acceptance before repo.commit runs, or document an explicit deviation if commit-then-/undo is the intended UX.

findings.summary: No approval gate distinguishes security-sensitive files (CI workflows, Dockerfiles, lockfiles, CODEOWNERS) from ordinary edits.
findings.description: The remit requires human approval for edits to security-sensitive files. allowed_to_edit applies one uniform confirmation with no path classification; once a file is in abs_fnames it returns True with no further gate.
findings.policy_rule_text: Edits to security-sensitive files such as container build files, CI/CD workflow definitions, dependency manifests and lockfiles, code-ownership files, and security-policy files.

evidence.snippet: allowed_to_edit applies one uniform 'Allow edits to file that has not been added to the chat?' confirmation; once a file is in abs_fnames it returns True (line 2198) with no further gate.
No path-classification logic exists for .github/workflows, Dockerfile, *.lock, or CODEOWNERS anywhere in scope.

recommended_actions: Add a sensitive-path classifier in base_coder.allowed_to_edit that forces an explicit_yes_required confirmation for CI/CD, container, dependency-manifest, lockfile, code-ownership, and security-policy files even when already added to the chat.

findings.summary: Gitignored and credential-bearing files can be added to the chat via flags or by being already tracked, with no risk acknowledgement.
findings.description: The remit requires explicit risk acknowledgement before ignored credential-bearing files enter context. --add-gitignore-files admits ignored files with an ordinary path and no credential-specific warning; there is no name-based detection of .env / key / credential files.
findings.policy_rule_text: Files excluded by the project's ignore rules MUST NOT be read unless the developer explicitly adds the file to the chat. / Aider MUST NEVER read ignored credential-bearing files — environment files, secrets, key and certificate files, credential and token files — unless the developer has explicitly added the file to the chat and acknowledged the risk.

evidence.snippet: gitignored files are skipped unless self.add_gitignore_files is set, which then adds them with an ordinary path and no credential-specific warning or risk acknowledgement.
cmd_add skips git-ignored files only when not self.coder.add_gitignore_files; there is no detection of credential-bearing names (.env, *.pem, credentials*) and no explicit risk-acknowledgement step.

recommended_actions: When --add-gitignore-files is used or a tracked file matches credential-bearing name patterns, require a distinct risk-acknowledgement confirmation in cmd_add / base_coder before the file enters context.

positives.title: Dependencies fully version-pinned
positives.description: requirements.txt pins every dependency with == via uv compile against requirements/common-constraints.txt, and pyproject.toml pins the supported Python range — a strong supply-chain baseline.

positives.title: Human-in-the-loop edit gate for un-added files
positives.description: Coder.allowed_to_edit requires a confirm_ask before creating a new file or editing a file not already added to the chat.

positives.title: LLM-suggested shell commands require explicit-yes confirmation
positives.description: handle_shell_commands gates model-suggested shell commands behind confirm_ask with explicit_yes_required=True before they run.

positives.title: Architect editor sub-coder runs with shell suggestions disabled
positives.description: ArchitectCoder sets suggest_shell_commands=False for the spawned editor coder, narrowing that execution path.

log_files.no_logs_note: No structured action or audit log was found in the scoped code; log_llm_history (opt-in plaintext prompt dump) and append_chat_history (markdown transcript) are debugging/UX artifacts, not action-level audit records — see finding PRAX-2026-05-19-005.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Ad hoc. Aider is a well-engineered, narrowly-scoped developer tool with genuinely strong supply-chain hygiene (fully pinned dependencies) and a real human-in-the-loop edit-confirmation step — but it treats the developer's terminal as a fully trusted environment and pushes nearly every safety boundary into prose. Repository containment, shell-command confirmation, untrusted-content handling, secret redaction, and action logging are all either absent or soft and globally defeatable via --yes-always. The Zero Trust score of 1 — the double-weighted category — is the dominant drag on the number and accurately reflects that the remit's hard prohibitions have no enforcement layer beneath them.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: Aider is purpose-built and narrowly scoped — a coding assistant with a fixed coder/edit-format inventory and no general-purpose framing — and the architect path explicitly disables shell suggestions for the editor sub-coder. But domain boundaries (repo containment, no package install) live only in prose; the tool inventory exposed via slash commands (/run, /git, /web, /load) is wider than the remit's read-propose-commit baseline, with no code gate restricting it.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: External content enters LLM context with zero validation: /web scraped pages, /run command output, repo file content, comments, and commit messages are all concatenated into messages with no trust labeling or injection screening — a scope-wide grep for injection/sanitiz/untrusted returns nothing. The remit explicitly requires injection-detection on comments, docstrings, and docs; it is absent.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: No code-level interposition on the agent's actions: cmd_run executes shell commands with no confirmation, confirm_ask is globally defeatable via --yes-always, auto_lint runs subprocesses unprompted after edits, read_text/write_text open any path with no repo-root containment, and /read-only accepts absolute paths anywhere on disk. LLM output flows to subprocess with shell=True. The one real control — the edit-confirmation gate for un-added files — is human-in-the-loop and bypassable.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: requirements.txt is fully version-pinned (==) and uv-compiled against a common-constraints.txt; pyproject.toml pins the Python range; no user credentials are stored in the scoped workspace files. Gaps: no ML-BOM/SBOM, the model/runtime is whatever the user configures with no integrity pinning, and versioncheck.py offers a pip self-upgrade path.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: A tests/ tree exists at repo root (out of scope), but within the scoped code there is no evidence of adversarial testing — no injection-test harness, no red-team fixtures, no design artifact showing security findings drove the architecture. For a tool that ingests untrusted repo content and executes shell commands, the absence of any prompt-injection testing is a material gap.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: No structured action logging anywhere in scope. log_llm_history is an optional plaintext debug dump of raw prompt/response text, append_chat_history is a human-readable markdown transcript, and analytics.py emits coarse product-telemetry events to third parties — none is an action-level, decision-level, durable audit record of which files were edited, which shell commands ran, or which paths were read.
